---
title: "Parseval's theorem"
---

::: {.hidden}
 \providecommand{\vv}[1]{{\mathbf{#1}}}
 \providecommand{\pd}[2]{{\frac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}}
 \providecommand{\pdd}[2]{{\frac{\partial^2 #1}{\partial #2^2}}}
 \providecommand{\dd}[2]{{\frac{\mathrm{d} #1}{\mathrm{d} #2}}}
 \providecommand{\ddd}[2]{{\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 #1}{\mathrm{d} #2^2}}}
:::

Fourier analysis (both series and transforms, which we will see in the next section) is a vast area of mathematics, and there are many theorems. We will only cover one of the most important ones here. 

Again, we use the convention that
$$
f(x) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \left[ a_n \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) + b_n \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) \right],
$$
where $L$ is the period of the function $f$.


## Parseval's theorem
Parseval's theorem states that the Fourier coefficients of a function $f$ satisfy
$$
\frac{1}{L} \int_0^L |f(x)|^2\,dx
= |a_0|^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^\infty (|a_n|^2 + |b_n|^2).
$$
This is a very important result, because it tells us that the Fourier coefficients contain all the information about the function $f$. In particular, it tells us that the sum of the squares of the Fourier coefficients is equal to the average value of the square of the function. This is a kind of "energy conservation" principle, which is very useful in many applications.

### Proof (Non-examinable)
Here we can again use the inner product. Assume the $a_n$ and $b_n$ are all real.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{L} \int_0^L |f(x)|^2 dx &= \frac{1}{L} \langle f(x), f(x) \rangle \\
&= \frac{1}{L} \left\langle a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \left[ a_n \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) + b_n \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) \right], a_0 + \sum_{m=1}^\infty \left[ a_m \cos\left(\frac{2\pi m x}{L}\right) + b_m \sin\left(\frac{2\pi m x}{L}\right) \right] \right\rangle \\
&= \frac{a_0^2}{L} \langle 1, 1 \rangle + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \left[ \frac{a_n^2}{L} \langle \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right), \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) \rangle + \frac{b_n^2}{L} \langle \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right), \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) \rangle \right] \\&= a_0^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^\infty (a_n^2 + b_n^2).
\end{aligned}
$$
where we have used the facts that $\langle 1, 1 \rangle = L$, $\langle \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right), \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) \rangle = \frac{L}{2}$, and $\langle \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right), \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{L}\right) \rangle = \frac{L}{2}$, with all the other inner products being zero.

## In exponential form
If we have a Fourier series in complex exponential form
$$f(x) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty c_n e^{i \frac{2\pi n x}{L}},$$
using
$$
a_0 = a_0,\quad
a_n = c_n+c_{-n}\ \text{ for } n>0,\quad
b_n = i(c_n-c_{-n})\ \text{ for } n>0,
$$
Parseval's theorem becomes
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{L} \int_0^L |f(x)|^2\,dx
&= |c_0|^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^\infty \left(|c_n+c_{-n}|^2 + |i(c_n-c_{-n})|^2\right)
\\&=|c_0|^2 + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \left(|c_n|^2 + |c_{-n}|^2\right)
\\&=\sum_{-\infty}^\infty |c_n|^2.
\end{aligned}
$$
